prompt: When capacity-planning a real-time push system, which metric matters most and why?
choices: Requests per second, because each push is a separate short request
Concurrent open connections, because each one pins server memory and a concurrency slot for its whole lifetime
Total bytes per response, because push responses are unusually large
Average CPU per request, because push has no connection state

prompt: A push system delivers events at-least-once, and clients reconnect frequently after network drops. Which measures keep this correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Make consumers idempotent so applying the same event twice is harmless
Expose a cursor / last-event-id so a client can resume from where it left off
Add sequence numbers and reorder client-side when ordering matters
Assume each event is delivered exactly once and skip duplicate handling

prompt: An internal dashboard shows background-job status that changes a few times an hour, viewed in always-open background tabs, with no millisecond-sensitive decisions. Why is periodic polling often the better choice than real-time push here?
choices: Because polling is stateless and cacheable, avoids pinning many idle connections, and recovers from a dropped network with the next tick — and the data isn't urgent enough to justify push's cost
Because polling always delivers data with lower latency than push
Because push cannot be used for status data at all
Because polling guarantees no event is ever missed under any circumstances
